---
draft: true
title: "Visualising water allocation time series in R with Plotly"
date: 2020-09-01
categories: [R, Visualisation, Water, Mexico]
description: "A short tutorial on creating interactive time series charts in R Markdown using Plotly, illustrated with historical agricultural water concession data for the Yucatán Peninsula."
image: ../assets/images/img-scatter.svg
---

This post walks through the steps for building an interactive time series chart in R using the Plotly package. The data is agricultural water concession records from CONAGUA's REPDA registry for the Yucatán Peninsula.

## Setup

```r
library(plotly)
library(dplyr)
library(widgetframe)
```

The data download follows the same process as the [Leaflet tutorial](2020-09-01-leaflet-maps-yucatan.html): download the CONAGUA ZIP file, read the CSV files, merge on a common identifier, and filter to the three peninsula states and agricultural use designation.

## Aggregation

Once the data is filtered, aggregate by registration date and usage type:

```r
df_summary <- df_clean |>
  group_by(date, uso) |>
  summarise(volume_m3 = sum(vol_anual, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
```

Date formatting matters here. The raw registration dates are character strings in various formats — cleaning them to a consistent `YYYY-MM-DD` structure before grouping avoids silent errors in the time axis.

## Plot

```r
fig <- plot_ly(df_summary,
  x = ~date, y = ~volume_m3,
  color = ~uso, type = "scatter", mode = "lines+markers") |>
  layout(
    title = "Agricultural water concessions — Yucatán Peninsula",
    xaxis = list(title = "Date", tickformat = "%b %Y"),
    yaxis = list(title = "Annual volume (m³)")
  )

frameWidget(fig)
```

The chart shows the evolution of water allocations over time by usage category. Agricultural concessions grow through the 1990s and 2000s alongside the expansion of export-oriented farming. The time series provides a demand-side baseline for the groundwater recharge modelling described in the [water metabolism project](../../projects/project-water-metabolism.html).

The main thing to watch for when rendering Plotly in R Markdown or Quarto is the `widgetframe::frameWidget()` wrapper — without it, interactive widgets sometimes fail to display correctly in certain output contexts.
